hook: Two districts still clear 5% net yield — here is where

slides.title: Beat 5% Net Yield
slides.subtitle: HDB & condo rentals in 2026 — the districts still printing landlord returns

slides.headline: Median CCR condo rent (2-bed) as at Q1 2026
slides.footnote: SRX / URA caveats Q1 2026. CCR 2-bed median psf rent eased ~3% q-o-q as expat demand moderated post-2025 peak.

slides.headline: Gross rental yield — mature-estate 4-room HDB
slides.footnote: HDB median resale prices vs SRX median HDB rents, mature estates (Bishan / Toa Payoh / Queenstown), Q1 2026.

slides.title: Gross Yield by District — Q1 2026

items.label: D19 (Punggol/Sengkang)
items.sub: New condo supply absorbed; strong young-family rental pool keeps rents sticky

items.label: D14 (Geylang/Eunos)
items.sub: Lower entry prices amplify yield; MRT uplift from CR Line pricing in slowly

items.label: D10 (Bukit Timah/Holland)
items.sub: Trophy addresses, high capital values compress yield despite premium rents

items.label: D1-4 (CCR Core)
items.sub: Expat pool shrinking; oversupply of luxury units dragging achievable rents down

slides.footnote: Gross yield = annualised median rent ÷ median transacted price. URA / SRX Q1 2026 caveats. Net yield approx 0.8–1.0pp lower after maintenance & property tax.

slides.title: 3 Reasons Outside-Central Yields Outperform
items: Entry prices in OCR are 35–45% below CCR for comparable unit sizes, so the same rent dollar buys more yield.
TEL and CR Line extensions are still pulling tenants — teachers, healthcare workers, young professionals — into D18/D19/D22 catchments.
HDB upgrader demand keeps OCR condo vacancy tight; SRX vacancy proxy for D19 condos sat below 6% in Q1 2026.

slides.title: What Eats Into That Gross Number
items: Property tax on non-owner-occupied residential: 12–36% of AV (Budget 2025 rates still in effect) — budget 0.5–0.8% yield drag.
Maintenance & sinking fund for a typical 1,000 sqft condo: $400–600/month — roughly 0.6% yield drag at median rent.
Agent commission + vacancy gap (1 month per 24): effectively another 0.2–0.3% drag annually.
Net-net: subtract ~1.0pp from gross to get real landlord return. Only D14 and D19 clear 4% net; only D19 touches 5% net in best-case scenarios.

slides.title: Chase Net Yield, Not Gross Bragging Rights
slides.body: Every landlord I speak to anchors on gross. The tax table and condo management fee are where returns go to die. D19 new launches bought at the right quantum — sub-$1.5M — still clear 4.2–4.8% net today. That is where smart money is repositioning while CCR landlords discount to fill units.
slides.quote: The best rental asset isn't the most expensive one — it's the one tenants actually want to live in.

caption:
Two Singapore districts still clear 5% net rental yield in 2026 — most landlords don't know which ones. 📈

• CCR median 2-bed condo rent slipped ~3% q-o-q as expat demand eased in Q1 2026
• D19 (Punggol/Sengkang) leads at 5.3% gross / ~4.5% net; D14 holds at 5.1% gross
• CCR core (D1–4) yields just 3.2% gross — net barely covers costs after tax and fees

If your investment property sits in the core, the numbers no longer work. Reposition before the tenant pool shrinks further.
